title: Kidneys: renal anatomy
body_html:
Surface anatomy of the kidneys
  • Posteriorly the kidneys occupy from T12 to L3 levels.
  • The hilum is typically found at L1 level i.e. in the transpyloric plane
  • The right kidney is lower than the left kidney
  • The kidneys move downwards by 2.5 cm on inspiration

illustration of the surface anatomy of the kidney. Note the right kidney sits lower than the left kidney as it is pushed down by the liver.

Layers covering the kidney
From deep -> superficilal:
  • Fibrous capsule
  • Perirenal fat
  • Renal fascia
  • Pararenal fat

illustration of the layers covering the kidney. The renal fascia is important as a ruptured kidney abscess will be contained within the fascia. Pus may point above the iliac crest posteriorly

Arterial supply to the kidneys
  • The renal arteries arise directly from the abdominal aorta at L1-L2 level posterior to the pancreas.
  • Renal arteries are end arteries. Obstruction leads to infarction.
  • Renal arteries divide into anterior and posterior branches then segmental branches before the segmental arteries enter the hilum which typically lies at L1
  • Renal arteries divide as below:

renal artery → segmental arteries→ lobar arteries → interlobar arteries → arcuate arteries


illustration of blood supply to the kidney

Lymph drainage of the kidneys
  • Para-aortic nodes

Innervation of the kidneys
  • From renal plexus which is formed by the coeliac plexus, sympathetic trunk and spinal nerves
  • Sensory afferents from the renal pelvis and ureter travel to T11-L2 hence the flank, loin and groin pain felt in renal colic. As the genitofemoral nerve originates from the upper L1-2 segments of the lumbar plexus pain may also be felt down the leg as pain is transmitted via the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve.

Anatomical relations of the kidneys

Right kidney
Direct contact or
Layer of peritoneum in-between		Anterior to kidneyPosterior to kidney
In direct contact
  • Diaphragm
  • Right adrenal gland
  • Duodenum
  • Colon
Layer of peritoneum in-between
  • Liver
  • Distal part of small intestine
  • Liver
  • Adrenal
  • Duodenum (2nd part)
  • Right colic flexure
  • Diaphragm
  • Costodiaphragmatic recess of pleura
  • 12th rib
  • Psoas, quadratus and transversus (medial to lateral)
  • Iliohypogastric and ilioinguinal nerves
    • - 12th thoracic nerve (subcostal nerve)

    Left kidney
    Direct contact or
    Layer of peritoneum in-between    		Anterior to kidneyPosterior to kidney
    In direct contact

    • Left adrenal gland
    • Pancreas
    • Colon
    Layer of peritoneum in-between
    • Stomach
    • Spleen
    • Distal part of small intestine

    Anatomy
    Abdomen & pelvis anatomy
    Surface anatomy of the kidneys
    • Posteriorly the kidneys occupy from T12 to L3 levels.
    • The hilum is typically found at L1 level i.e. in the transpyloric plane
    • The right kidney is lower than the left kidney
    • The kidneys move downwards by 2.5 cm on inspiration

    illustration of the surface anatomy of the kidney. Note the right kidney sits lower than the left kidney as it is pushed down by the liver.

    Layers covering the kidney
    From deep -> superficilal:
    • Fibrous capsule
    • Perirenal fat
    • Renal fascia
    • Pararenal fat

    illustration of the layers covering the kidney. The renal fascia is important as a ruptured kidney abscess will be contained within the fascia. Pus may point above the iliac crest posteriorly

    Arterial supply to the kidneys
    • The renal arteries arise directly from the abdominal aorta at L1-L2 level posterior to the pancreas.
    • Renal arteries are end arteries. Obstruction leads to infarction.
    • Renal arteries divide into anterior and posterior branches then segmental branches before the segmental arteries enter the hilum which typically lies at L1
    • Renal arteries divide as below:

    renal artery → segmental arteries→ lobar arteries → interlobar arteries → arcuate arteries


    illustration of blood supply to the kidney

    Lymph drainage of the kidneys

    Innervation of the kidneys
    • From renal plexus which is formed by the coeliac plexus, sympathetic trunk and spinal nerves
    • Sensory afferents from the renal pelvis and ureter travel to T11-L2 hence the flank, loin and groin pain felt in renal colic. As the genitofemoral nerve originates from the upper L1-2 segments of the lumbar plexus pain may also be felt down the leg as pain is transmitted via the femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve.
